course_description: This course focuses on the legal environments affecting international business transactions & how managers can use knowledge of these environments to gain competitive advantage. It addresses the use of legal information at the corporate planning level as well as the minimisation of legal risks in contractual & other day-to-day business dealings. It shows students how managers may use knowledge of the domestic, international & target country legal environments to support, defend & transform the firm, thus maximising its competitiveness.

requirements.assumed_background: No previous study of law is necessary, but a lot of law will be covered in the lectures. Law will be covered at both a conceptual and literal level to form a level of knowledge that can be applied to factual scenarios to enable business decision makers to identify and assess risk in a firm’s international business transactions and investments. Before attempting this course, students are advised that it is important to complete the appropriate prerequisite course(s) listed on the front of this course profile. No responsibility will be accepted by the School of Business, the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law or The University of Queensland for poor student performance occurring in courses where the appropriate prerequisite(s) has/have not been completed, for any reason whatsoever.

Additional Timetable Information
Please note : Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email business.mytimetable@uq.edu.au from your UQ student email account with the following details:

Full name,

Student ID, and

the Course Code

The course timetable can be viewed via the Learning Resources page on the IBUS3306 Blackboard site.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The course aims are to inform students of the importance of the legal environment for successful international business and provide students with the ability to work with an understanding of the international legal environment and make appropriate decisions to identify risks and seek competitive advantage.

learning_outcomes.description: Know and integrate the differences in the international legal environment when conducting the risk analysis associated with successful international business practice.
Identify, analyse and evaluate the impact of differences in international law when entering into international business transactions.
Acknowledge, critically explain and make appropriate recommendations based on how differences in international legal systems impact on international business practice.
Have a working and practical level of knowledge of the implications of contract law on international business practice.
Identify a working range of legal hazards and risks required to be acknowledged and integrated into business risk planning.

assessment_details.title: In-Semester Exam
assessment_details.due_date: 23 Mar 24 - 20 Apr 24; Centrally timetabled exam on one of the following days: 23/3/2024, 13/4/2024 and 20/4/2024.
assessment_details.task_description:
This In-Semester Exam will be an invigilated exam administered via Inspera.

The exam will be based on a choice of multiple response answers/multiple choice selections to 5 factual scenarios and/or stimulus. Each answer will be worth 1 mark. You will need to identify the issue from the facts given or stimulus material provided; demonstrate your knowledge and apply it to the questions relevant to each of the 5 factual scenarios by selecting and making the correct/most correct/true /false response. Each factual scenario has 8 questions.

Exams submitted late, without prior approval from the Course Coordinator, will incur a late penalty under the latest UQ assessment policies.

If you are unable to sit your exam, you must apply for a deferred exam. The deferred mid semester exam for all Saturday mid-semester exams will be announced by Centrral Examinations when the adjusted Academic Calendar is announced.

Students are advised that the use of AI technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.task_description:
This will be a 120-minute Final Exam, plus additional 10 minutes reading time.

The Final Exam will be an invigilated exam administered via Inspera.

The Final Exam will cover lectures 2 - 13 and will consist of MCQs, Multiple Response to Stimulus and/or short answer questions you will be required to respond to in the time available.

The exam will be held during the central examination period at a day/time set and notified by the University's Central Examinations unit.

Please refer to section 5.3 regarding penalties for late submission of your Final Exam.

If you are unable to sit your exam, you must apply for a deferred exam (see section 5.4).

Further details about the exam will be discussed in class and posted to our course Blackboard site.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Viewing an Exam

For information on viewing an end of semester exam, please refer to: Exams and assessment advice

Re-marks

For information on the re-mark process, please refer to: Querying a result

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

Computing Facilities

Courses offered by UQ Business School utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskUs, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. https://web.library.uq.edu.au/contact-us

Professional Code of Conduct

The University of Queensland has strict guidelines around academic integrity, plagiarism, and assessment which should be carefully read in the policy sections of this eCP. In addition, UQ Business School expects that as future business leaders and entrepreneurs, our students will adhere to the highest standards of ethical behaviour in all of their coursework.

Attendance at lectures, tutorials, seminars and workshops is expected and students are reminded that lecture recordings are not designed to replace in-class interactions and learning experiences.

It is a common courtesy to your fellow students and teaching staff to ensure you are on time for all class sessions, that you come prepared and are ready and willing to actively participate in class activities. Leaving class early is also disruptive to your fellow students and lecturers/tutors.

Please use professional etiquette and courtesy in all of your written and oral communication with teaching staff and fellow students. Please also ensure that all written correspondence includes your name and student ID.

Another matter of etiquette is the use of phones, laptops and other electronic devices in class. Some in-class activities may require the use of devices but otherwise we expect that these will be switched off during class time.

Students are not to make their own recordings of lectures or tutorials as this contravenes the UQ policy on Student Integrity and Misconduct. This includes taking photos of lecture or tutorial slides and/or other materials. Teaching staff will distribute all required materials to you via Blackboard.

Course Coordinators and lecturers may set additional requirements and expectations for their respective courses.

Other Course Guidelines

University of Queensland's values and guiding principles

The University of Queensland Business School is committed to establishing and sustaining a community that supports independent scholarly learning, critical judgment, academic integrity and ethical standards in all students.

As a member of the University community, we also expect students to accept shared responsibility, conducting themselves in a manner consistent with the University’s values and guiding principles.

In accordance with the Student Charter and Student Integrity & Misconduct policy , students must ensure they:

(a) are familiar and compliant with the expectations and responsibilities prescribed under the student charter ;

(b) submit only work that is their own or that properly acknowledges the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of others;

(c) avoid lending original work to others for any reason;

(d) comply with assessment conditions for policies and procedures, rules and regulations;

(e) be clear about what is appropriate referencing and the consequences of inappropriate referencing in their discipline;

(f) do not collude with people, including fellow students and external third-party services, when completing assessment work;

(g) complete the academic integrity online tutorial within the first semester of study at the University prior to the census date.

If you do require support with your studies, please contact your Course Coordinator, tutor or UQ Student Services .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course introduces students to the legal environments involved in facilitating international business and trade and how those legal systems affect international trade and business transactions. The course will impart knowledge of legal environments to enable business managers to be aware of potential issues, their impact on the business and to anticipate and consider what risk mitigant might be available that can also maintain a competitive advantage. It addresses the need to use a Business Legal Strategy and to gather relevant legal information apposite to the business activities in contractual & other day-to-day business dealings. It shows students how managers may use knowledge of the various legal environments (domestic, international) to support, defend and transform the firm’s exposure to risk, creating the conditions to maximise competitiveness within the firm’s risk profile for its business dealings.

This ‘course aims at giving students an appreciation of the risks associated with the complexity of the international legal environment. The course covers a broad spectrum of issues faced by organisations when entering into international transactions relating to goods and services and direct private foreign investment. It will show how to identify legal hazards in order to assess and mitigate risk with the firm’s legal advisors, as well as show how to use legal knowledge to support, defend and transform the firm in its international business operations. It is recommended for business students as well as law students wanting a practical application of law to business.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This course has been updated and redesigned as a result of changes in 2020 - 2023 to the international trade rules governing the sale of goods and business considerations involved in managing risk, contractual obligations, performance, and liability as a result of the impact of COVID – 19 and regional conflicts on the firm's risk profile in conducting international business and trade. It has also been updated to cover the evolving use of sanctions by national laws and international organisations e.g., UN Sanctions; and how the risk of non-compliance can be approached by the firm.

Student feedback for IBUS3306 is regularly reviewed as part of the Business school's ongoing quality assurance program and, where necessary, changes are implemented in response. One such recent change relates to the text. In previous years, the text was compulsory. Due, however, to the speed of change in many national laws and in international law, it was recognised that a text would be outdated and not up to date enough to be reliable. The text for the course has now been made optional but is highly recommended. Much of the primary course material being provided in lectures or lecture slides via case references or links is embedded in course slides (e.g., links to actual legislation or conventions etc). This ensures that the material presented remains current and relevant. Students have noted the value of this approach in the past and, as such, this approach will be again used.
other_learning_activities_information:
Lectures (On Campus) & Tutorials - Practicums (Discussions)

IBUS3306 will be conducted In Person Only on Campus via 3-hour contact time per week. The structure of the contact time will be in two parts. The first part will generally be 2 hours Lectures (L2) and generally follow content/concept delivery. The Lectures are recorded . The second part being the 1-hour Tutorial - practicum (T1) in the form of discussions and will involve a review of the previous weeks Quiz (if it is after the census date) and/or Tutorial which combines both theory and application of an aspect of the current week Lecture content. The application of knowledge delivered in Lectures to the practicum exercises will be based on real-life examples of the application of the theory in everyday business practice. This second part involving the application of knowledge to the practicum exercises will also provide highly relevant guidance for the assessment items in the course. Student participation is highly encouraged in the second part, 1-hour Tutorial - practicum (T1) because this is your opportunity to apply the knowledge yourselves. The Tutorials are not recorded so all feel comfortable and not self-conscious when asking any relevant question and to promote a positive and spontaneous engaging process for individuals to gain new understanding and learning from different perspectives of other students. The 1-hour Tutorial - practicum (T1) will be scheduled as required based on numbers enrolled and students will be allocated to a tutorial group by the Business School timetabling team. All timetabling preferences and necessary adjustments throughout the semester will be considered based on available places. All timetabling adjustment requests (and reasons with relevant supporting data) must be made in the first instance to the Business School timetabling team not the Course Coordinator or Lecturer.

Student Responsibility

Note that the primary forum for communication and correspondence will be via the Announcements tab in Blackboard https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to attend Lectures and Tutorial Practicums at the times scheduled and allocated to you; consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Course materials

Course materials including lecture and tutorial material, assessment information and other administrative announcements will be posted on Blackboard under the Announcements tab.

Consultation

Teaching staff are there to assist you. Staff members have set consultation times during which you may request an appointment by email with your question/matter. Consultation times for staff are posted on the course Blackboard site under Announcements and updated regularly. Consultations outside of these times is exceptional and by appointment only. The Tutorials each week should be considered the first opportunity to ask questions on the previous weeks content at the time the Quiz is being discussed (after the census date).

If you have more specific questions or points that require clarification, you are expected to be prepared for any Consultation. If staff members are unavailable send them an email in advance and efforts will be made to reschedule it. A well-prepared consultation following your email identifying the question/matter to be discussed, will often require an explanation in consultation of your own analysis or application of the relevant content and should last approx 15 minutes.

Where applicable, you may also use the course's weekly Tutorials - Discussions to seek assistance from both your peers and staff.

Administration

Tutorial allocations and change requests must be made in the first instance to the Business School timetabling team not the Course Coordinator or Lecturer. All timetabling adjustment requests (and reasons with relevant supporting data) must be made in the first instance to the Business School timetabling team not the Course Coordinator or Lecturer. Questions related to course content should, in the first instance be directed to the tutor/s involved in delivery of the Tutorial (Discussion) each week. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the tutor should then be referred to the course co-ordinator/associate lecturer. Administrative questions about this course (e.g., exam viewing, etc) should be addressed to the associate lecturer but please check the Announcement tab of the Blackboard site first. Questions regarding exam/assignment format and content should be directed to the course coordinator. General administrative questions (for example, centrally timetabled exams, release of online exams or room venues if offered in internal mode, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Reception and Student Administration: Level 2 Colin Clark Building #39, St Lucia Campus or info@business.uq.edu.au

Professional Ethics

You must have completed the University of Queensland - Academic Integrity Module before starting this course. The principle of integrity imposes an obligation on all professionals (e.g. business people, lawyers and accountants) to be straightforward and honest in all professional and business relationships. Integrity also implies fair dealing and truthfulness. The principle of objectivity imposes an obligation on professionals not to compromise their professional judgment because of bias, conflict of interest, or the undue influence of others. In other words, professionals should not subordinate their judgment to the views of others. (APES 110 & 120). Your completion of the University Academic Integrity Module is a prerequisite to doing any assessment in this course. You will be required to make an Academic Integrity Pledge by way of declaration in each Assessment that will bind your conduct in relation to the Assessment, the use of your computer, the course material, your assessments and responses. Breach of any Academic integrity Pledge will be taken seriously, and the consequences will potentially attack severe penalties reflected in your academic transcript an may affect your integrity and or employability.

It is a breach of Copyright to use self-recorded or University transcripts or any course materials in any form of AI or large language model or for any purpose including trading them online for other course materials. The use of AI is not permitted in this course. The use of course materials and recordings by you in any form of AI is not permitted. You are not permitted to upload or input any course materials in any format including (screenshots and recordings) into any Generative AI or large language model or use course materials in data trading sites or platforms that ask for summaries of course material and doing so will be considered misconduct. You can only use the course materials to study this course for the assessments in the Semester you are enrolled or as otherwise permitted by University policy. This course covers legal rules, analysis and case law and these change and evolve. Therefore, after your enrolment ceases or you have submitted your assessments reliance on the individual presenter’s views and materials provided must not be used or relied upon in any factual scenario and must not be used in substitution of obtaining legal advice on any fact, matter or circumstance from a qualified legal practitioner licensed to practise in the relevant jurisdiction.

In the context of the university, "plagiarism" is a failure to act with integrity and objectivity. Plagiarism is a broad term referring to the practice of appropriating someone else's ideas or work and presenting them as your own without acknowledgement. Plagiarism is literary or intellectual theft. Plagiarism and theft of intellectual property rights (.e.g. ideas or copyright material) unlawful and actionable at law. These forms of intellectual theft and/or breach of copyright can take a number of forms, including:

Copying the work of another student, whether that student is in the same class, from an earlier year of the same course, or form another tertiary institution altogether.

Copying any section, no matter how brief, from a book, journal, article or other written source, without duly acknowledging it as a quotation.

Copying any map, diagram, or table of figures without duly acknowledging the source;

Copying (by screenshot, photo or any other method) any Assessment you are to do as part of your evaluation in this course;

Taking screen shots or photos of screens displaying course content or providing to any translator or other third party or entity any course material;

Selling, trading or providing to any other person or entity any part of the course material or your derivative material prepared based on it, assessments or your responses for their use or trade; or

Paraphrasing or otherwise using the ideas of another author without duly acknowledging the source.

Whatever the form, plagiarism and theft of intellectual property rights is unacceptable both academically and professionally. By plagiarising or unlawful use of intellectual property rights you are stealing the work of another person and if including it in your submissions to Assessments you are cheating by representing it as your own.

Cheating means to defraud or swindle. Students who seek to gain an advantage by unfair means such as copying another student's work, or selling or trading the course material or your notes in any forum (in person or online) or in any other way misleading the lecturer about your knowledge or ability or the amount of work you have done, are guilty of cheating.

Students who condone plagiarism and cheating by allowing their work or assessment responses to be copied are equally guilty of plagiarism and cheating.

Any instances of plagiarism and cheating or unlawful/non permitted use of intellectual property rights can therefore be expected to draw potentially severe penalties.

4.3 Guide To A Rewarding Learning Experience In IBUS3306

The course requires about 10 hours of work per week. Lectures and tutorials account for about 3 hours. This leaves approx. 7 hours per week for studying the recommended textbook, learning material, completing each Quiz Online and participating in the online tutorial discussions. Before enrolling, think about whether you can make this commitment.

Make sure you check your student email and the course's Blackboard website regularly (at least every few days). You are deemed to have received any message sent to your UQ email address. When contacting UQ staff, use your UQ email address.

LECTURES (On Campus)

All lectures are recorded in the assigned lecture theatre by the University. Recordings will be posted to Blackboard using Echo360. It will usually take approximately 24 hours for the Lecture Recording to be available in the Blackboard site under the Learning Resources tab and folder titled Lecture Recordings. Best results will come from prioritising your time to be at the online or on campus lecture when it is run so you can use the recordings later to clarify or enhance your study of the material. It is not recommended that you just look at the lecture recording in lieu of attending the on campus lecture as you will fall behind quickly because the lecture content go together and the pace we cover the content picks up speed progressively week on week. Knowledge is layered each week so you need to keep current to keep developing your knowledge covered in previous lectures. Staying on top of the content weekly will put you in the best position to gain the highest potential marks in the quizzes. As later lecture content builds on previous lecture content, catching up becomes a difficult task as you will not have covered the building blocks for the layering of knowledge or critical analysis required in subsequent lectures. The Lecture power point slides are generally released at 8:30 am on the Friday prior to the relevant teaching week and downloadable on Blackboard subject to the University's permitted use of its intellectual property rights and derivative material created by you using it as specified in the blackboard site. The release timing may change due to public holidays in the Academic Calendar. The power point slides and what is said in Lectures in addressing the power point slides are examinable. The recommended reading text is highly recommended and covers in more detail matters that are examinable - it often can provide you with an overview of the content covered and save you spending your time looking for consultations unnecessarily. Other materials are accessible by link within the lecture power point slides, or posted on Blackboard. Required readings and materials covered in tutorials are also examinable.

TUTORIALS (Discussions)

There are In Person Only Weekly 1-hour Tutorials - Practicums (T1) which commence in teaching week 2 of the Academic Calendar. You are expected to register for and attend one Tutorial per week from Week 2 of the Academic Calendar. You are required to select your preferred class time through My Timetable and must attend your allocated time slot (if more than one class time is offered) as numbers per Tutorial will be limited. The Week 2 tutorial online will cover a practicum and part of it will be unstructured and used to orientate you in the course. Please direct all tutorial allocation enquiries and request to the Business School timetabling team who are responsible for making all allocations and approved adjustment.

Tutorials are not recorded. This is so all feel comfortable and not self-conscious when asking any relevant question and to promote a positive and spontaneous engaging process for individuals to gain new understanding and learning from different perspectives of other students. If a tutorial has to be held exceptionally via Zoom for any reason it is recommended that you locate yourself in an environment (not a library) where you can actively participate by switching on and using your camera and microphone in the tutorial rather then the chat room feature which has limitations on your engagement.

The internal mode is only offered this Semester so students have to enrol in a face-to-face tutorial session through mySI-net. Once enrolled in a tutorial session, the student is required to attend that assigned session throughout the semester unless they are granted permission to attend a different session.

Tutorials are usually based on material covered in that teaching week's lecture and/or in the recommended reading. Check the course's Blackboard website under "Learning Resources" to download the week's tutorial questions/practicums (if applicable to that Week) or see the last pages of the teaching week's Lecture Power Point Slides for the tutorial questions/practicums to be discussed in all tutorials following the lecture. It also identifies the particular chapters of the Recommended Reading text for that week’s content if applicable. Answers when relevant to practicums are posted by the following week in the weekly folder under Learning Resources in Blackboard, but some may be brief as the knowledge forming the basis for reasoning and application can be found in that weeks' Lecture Slides.

WEEKLY QUIZZES (ONLINE)

The weekly Quiz is first released in Week 2 of the Academic Calendar and each weekly Quiz looks to assess your knowledge and understanding of the previous weeks lecture content. They are to be completed in approximately 30 to 40 minutes (including reading time).

For example: Quiz 1 is released in Week 2 after the last scheduled Tutorial in Week 2. The Quiz assesses your knowledge and understanding of the lecture content covered in Week 1. That will usually mean you have a 1 week grace period to study and get on top of the lecture content and recommended reading in the Lecture Slides, Lecture Recording and/or review the recorded lecture again before doing the Online Quiz assessment. If you cannot do a Quiz in any one week don't panic as you get an opportunity to do 12 Quizzes in the Semester. You are only assessed on the best 10 of those 12 (if you submit all 12) - so there is still an ability for you to attain your fullest potential marks from the Quizzes if you prioritise doing them progressively each week. No extensions are granted for Quiz submissions. The Quizzes are a means for you to stay on top of the knowledge and gain marks progressively. They are also intended to embed with you the knowledge, weekly, so it can be built upon as we progress through the content later in the semester which will assume an understanding of the content covered earlier in the semester. Doing the Quizzes will also make you familiar with the style of questions for this redesigned Course in the Mid Semester and Final Exam. Past papers will not be a useful guide as the content and form of assessment has been updated.

CONSULTATION: FEEDBACK FROM LECTURER AND TUTORS

Consultation sessions are your chance to speak directly with the teaching staff, one-to-one online. A schedule of who is available when and where will be posted on the course's Blackboard website under "Course Help" and/or 'Announcements". Please email the Lecturer with your question/matter and analysis of the issues first and if it can be answered by reply email it will be responded to that way in the first instance. If the question/matter would best be addressed in a Zoom consultation session, you will receive an email reply with an appointment time in the next available consultation session. Please indicate in your initial email a time within the session times offered in the latest Announcement section of Blackboard that best suites you and every effort will be made to book that time unless your question/matter is responded to by email. If your preferred time is not possible the next available time period will be offered and sent to you by a Zoom meeting invitation. Don’t forget - you can always raise questions in the weekly Tutorial - Practicum (Discussion).

We expect that you are allocating enough hours per week to the course - viewing online lectures, preparing and actively engaging in tutorials, reading the recommended readings/textbook etc. If it becomes obvious that you have not met this commitment, you may be asked to return to the consultation later. If you fall too far behind, it is unlikely that you will understand the answers to your questions (hence the need for you to prepare to make the most out of the consultation time).

If you have personal or health problems that disrupt your study, contact in the first instance Student Services who can help you with administrative issues. They also offer programs to improve study techniques. Waiting queues may be long, so please seek advice earlier, rather than later. Collect documentation, such as medical appointments or reports, to support your need for assistance. Consult with Student Services at the earliest possible time to address any personal or health problems that disrupt your study.

late_submission:
Late submission of assessment/Extensions to assessment due date

A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances.

Please refer to my.UQ for information on applying for assessment extension: Applying for an assessment extension

Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .

Students applying for an extension must submit a request through my.UQ and provide evidence of their circumstances, as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than the assessment item submission due date and time.

Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date and time must include evidence of the reasons for the late request and will require the decision maker listed in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedures to accept the request for consideration.

In the Business School, all extension requests are processed by the Business School Exams Team.

Unless there is pedagogical reason provided in the ECP regarding limitations on extensions, Business School extensions will generally be limited to one week in the first instance.

In exceptional circumstances, approved extensions may be granted for more than one week but will not exceed four weeks in total. Where a student is incapacitated for a period exceeding four weeks of the teaching period, they might be advised to apply for removal of course.

Penalties for late assessment submission

The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).

Late period recorded from time submission is due

Penalty (see clause 2 for alternatives)

First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof)

10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item.

For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks

More than 7 periods of 24 hours

100%

Late submission of exams

Penalties for late submission will apply to the total mark available for an online (digital) examination unless a student can demonstrate system and/or process issues beyond their control resulted in the late submission of the examination.

Penalties for late submission are outlined in the University’s assessment policy. Please refer to: Examinations .
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.

Other Supplementary Assessment Information

Section 5.3 above "Late submission of assessment/Extensions to assessment due date" does not apply to Quiz Assessments.

Other

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise advised by your course coordinator, all written assignments are to be electronically submitted using Learn.UQ (Blackboard) . There are no assignments currently required in the Assessment section of this ECP

IMPORTANT: Online Submission

After submitting, ensure you view your Turnitin Digital Receipt or Learn.UQ confirmation number, and retain this number as proof of submission.

It is your responsibility to ensure the assignment has been successfully submitted. Any requests to waive the late submission penalty without proof of submission will not be considered under any circumstances.

Please note, emailing of assessment is not an acceptable form of submission.

Comprehensive submission instructions can be found on the UQ Library website, in the Learn.UQ Assessment section.

Turnitin Submission Instructions

Blackboard Assignment Submission Instructions

DEFERRED EXAMS

If you are unable to sit an exam, you must apply for a deferred exam no later than 5 days after the original examination.

Please refer to my.UQ for information on deferring an exam: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam

Further information on deferred examinations can be found in the Examinations Procedure: https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.11-examinations

Deferred in-semester exam requests are processed by the School. End of semester exams are processed Centrally.
